title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore the history and significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a vital waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu. They will learn about the traditional practices of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) that have protected this awa for generations and examine contemporary challenges facing the stream, including pollution and urban development impacts.
learningObjectives: Analyze how traditional Māori knowledge (mātauranga Māori) informs sustainable management practices for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Evaluate the impact of human activities on the health and mauri (life force) of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream using local environmental data.
Collaborate with local kaumātua and community experts to develop a kaitiakitanga action plan that addresses a current issue facing the awa.
Communicate findings and proposed solutions effectively using both oral presentation and digital storytelling methods.

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Connecting to the Stream
activities.description: Students begin with a karakia and waiata led by a local kaumātua at the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They hear the pūrākau (traditional story) of the stream's creation and significance to Ngāti Kahungunu, followed by a guided walk to identify native flora and fauna (e.g., kōura, tuna) and discuss their cultural importance.

activities.title: He Aha Te Rongoā? Investigating Stream Health
activities.description: Using water testing kits and observation guides, students collect data on water quality (pH, clarity, nitrate levels) at multiple points along the awa. They compare their findings with historical data provided by Hawke's Bay Regional Council and identify trends or areas of concern, linking these to land use practices in the catchment.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero a Ngā Kaumatua: Wisdom from Elders
activities.description: Students prepare and conduct interviews with local kaumātua and representatives from Manaaki Te Awa Trust, focusing on traditional kaitiakitanga practices, changes they have observed in the stream over time, and their visions for its future. Interviews are recorded for later analysis and presentation.

activities.title: Te Mahere Mahi: Designing a Kaitiakitanga Plan
activities.description: In small groups, students synthesize their data and interview insights to develop a practical kaitiakitanga action plan. Plans must include specific, achievable actions (e.g., organizing a community planting day, creating educational resources for locals, proposing riparian fencing initiatives) and identify relevant stakeholders and resources needed for implementation.

resources: Manaaki Te Awa Trust representatives and local Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'State of the Environment' reports and water quality data sets for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Digital storytelling tools (e.g., Adobe Spark, Canva) and audio recording equipment for interviews
Water testing kits and riparian zone health assessment guides from NZ Landcare Trust

tasks: Group presentation of kaitiakitanga action plan to a panel including kaumātua and trust representatives
Individual reflective journal documenting learning journey and personal connection to the awa
Digital story (3-5 minutes) synthesizing key findings and advocating for the stream's protection

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L5: Understand how cultural practices vary but reflect similar purposes
Social Studies L5: Understand how people’s management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Social Studies L5: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people’s lives
Social Studies L5: Understand how people define and seek human rights
